claims.text: The GAA c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu/p.D645E) variant is described as the most frequent pathogenic variant associated with infantile-onset Pompe disease (IOPD) in Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations of Han ancestry, and is classified as pathogenic.

citations.quote: the GAA c.1935C>A transversion in exon 14, which results in the p.Asp645Glu (p.D645E) missense mutation, is the most frequent pathogenic variant associated with IOPD in the Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations of Han ancestry, but is not frequently reported in any other region

claims.text: The GAA c.1935C>A (p.D645E) variant represents 36–80% of mutations in Taiwanese IOPD patients and is associated with a founder effect in Southern Han Chinese populations.

citations.quote: In Taiwanese populations, this c.1935C>A variant represents 36–80% of mutations11·12, and occur in context of a specific haplotype with conserved polymorphic markers linked to Taiwanese Pompe IOPD patients comparing to normal individuals. This may suggest the existence of a founder effect stemming from a diaspora of Southern Han Chinese to Taiwan and other locations

claims.text: Human IOPD caused by homozygous GAA c.1935C>A presents neonatally with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality.

citations.quote: causes infantile-onset Pompe disease (IOPD), presenting neonatally with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality

claims.text: GAA enzymatic activity in homozygous GAA c.1935C>A patient fibroblasts was previously measured at 0.08–0.82% of normal range for controls.

citations.quote: This aligns with observed levels of low GAA enzyme activity (0.08–0.82% of normal range for control) previously measured in homozygous GAA c.1935C>A patient fibroblasts25

claims.text: A CRISPR-Cas9 knock-in mouse model (Gaa em1935C>A) homozygous for the c.1935C>A (p.D645E) variant was generated and showed near-abolished GAA enzymatic activity (~1% of WT) in heart, skeletal muscle, and brain tissue.

citations.quote: KI mice had normal Gaa mRNA levels with significantly reduced level of GAA hydrolysis activity (about 1% of WT) in heart and skeletal muscle, as well as brain tissue

claims.text: Homozygous Gaa em1935C>A knock-in mice showed normal Gaa mRNA expression levels, indicating the c.1935C>A missense mutation does not affect mRNA levels (no nonsense-mediated decay).

citations.quote: The comparative ΔCt between mouse Gaa and housekeeping gene Gapdh acquired by RT-PCR among WT, HET, and KI groups are almost identical, indicating the Gaa c.1935C>A mutation does not affect Gaa mRNA levels

claims.text: Heterozygous (HET) Gaa em1935C>A mice had approximately 50% of WT GAA enzymatic activity with no other phenotypic differences from WT, consistent with one functional allele.

citations.quote: the HET group had close to 50% of the level of enzymatic activity observed in the WT group in each muscle tissue and brain tissue sample tested, indicating that the one WT allele produced functional enzyme, but not the c.1935C>A allele

claims.text: Gaa c.1935C>A KI C2C12 myoblast cells displayed less than 2.3% of WT GAA enzymatic activity and increased PAS staining indicating glycogen accumulation.

citations.quote: GAA enzymatic activity was almost abolished in Gaa c.1935C>A KI cells relative to Gaa wt cells; less than 2.3% of WT GAA activity was detected in the KI cell line

claims.text: Three-month-old homozygous Gaa em1935C>A knock-in mice demonstrated hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (increased IVSd, LVPWd, LVMI, and fractional shortening) and skeletal muscle weakness (reduced forelimb grip strength in males).

citations.quote: Increases in interventricular septal diameter (IVSd), LV posterior wall diameter (LVPWd), and LV mass index (LVMI) were observed in KI mice, compared to WT and/or HET mice (Fig. 6B), indicating pronounced hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
the male Gaaem1935C>A KI mouse showed a significant reduction (~19%) in normalized peak tension force compared to WT mice, indicating decreased forelimb muscle strength in KI mice

claims.text: Homozygous Gaa em1935C>A KI mice showed markedly increased glycogen storage in heart, diaphragm, and gastrocnemius muscle tissues, but only a non-significant increase in brain glycogen, unlike KO mice which showed significant elevation in all tissues.

citations.quote: KI mice showed a significant elevation of glycogen levels in muscle tissues, but no significant elevation in brain. The amount of glycogen was normalized to the amount of sample protein

claims.text: Gaa em1935C>A KI mice developed hypertrophic cardiomyopathy at approximately two months of age, which became quite marked at three months of age, but no premature mortality was observed.

citations.quote: Gaaem1935C>A mice developed hypertrophic cardiomyopathy at approximately two months of age, which becomes quite marked at three months of age. This muscle weakness phenotype may be due to a combination of sequelae from cardiomyopathy, impairment of lysosomal-autophagosomal fusion into autolysosomes, and catabolism of myofibril contractile proteins

claims.text: A significant divergence between the Gaa em1935C>A KI mouse model and human GAA c.1935C>A IOPD is the lack of infantile/neonatal mortality in the mouse model despite near-zero GAA activity.

citations.quote: A significant divergence of the model from human GAA c.1935C>A IOPD is the lack of infantile mortality in KI mice. This KI mouse, along with the Gaaem1826dupA KI mouse strain previously generated in our laboratory15 and other previously published Gaa KO models18·26·27, all demonstrate null or nearly-zero GAA enzyme activity. Nevertheless, no neonatal mortality has been observed in any model, while neonatal death is the inevitable clinical outcome in untreated IOPD patients

claims.text: The GAA c.1935C>A (p.D645E) variant is also reported as the most common mutation in Thai patients with infantile-onset Pompe disease (cited reference: Amarinthnukrowh et al. 2010).

citations.quote: Amarinthnukrowh, P. et al. p.D645E of acid α-glucosidase is the most common mutation in Thai patients with infantile-onset Pompe disease. Genet. Test Mol. Biomark. 14(6), 835–837 (2010)
